title: Norman and Medieval England
overview:
In 1066, one of the most famous dates in British history, William, Duke of Normandy (in northern France), invaded England. He defeated King Harold II at the Battle of Hastings and became known as William the Conqueror. The Norman Conquest transformed England completely. William introduced the feudal system, where the king owned all the land and granted it to nobles in return for military service. Norman French became the language of the royal court and government, mixing with Anglo-Saxon English to create the language we use today.

William ordered a great survey of England called the Domesday Book (1086), which recorded who owned every piece of land and what it was worth. The Normans built impressive stone castles across the country, including the Tower of London, to control their new territory. Over time, Norman and Anglo-Saxon cultures merged to form a new English identity.

The Middle Ages (medieval period) saw major developments in law and government. In 1215, King John was forced to sign the Magna Carta, a document that limited the king's power and established that everyone, including the monarch, was subject to the law. This was a crucial step toward democracy. Parliament gradually developed as a place where nobles and later common people could have a say in government. The period also saw the Hundred Years' War with France (1337-1453), including the famous English victory at the Battle of Agincourt in 1415. In 1348, the Black Death (bubonic plague) arrived in Britain, killing about one-third of the population and changing society forever by creating labor shortages that gave ordinary people more power.
key_facts: 1066: William, Duke of Normandy, invaded England and defeated King Harold II at the Battle of Hastings.
William became known as William the Conqueror and was crowned King of England.
The Norman Conquest transformed English society, language, and government.
The feudal system meant the king owned all land and granted it to nobles for military service.
The Domesday Book (1086) was a great survey recording land ownership across England.
Norman French became the language of the court, mixing with Anglo-Saxon to create modern English.
The Normans built stone castles across England, including the Tower of London.
1215: King John signed the Magna Carta, limiting the king's power.
The Magna Carta established that everyone, including the monarch, is subject to the law.
Parliament developed during the Middle Ages as a place for nobles to advise the king.
The Hundred Years' War with France lasted from 1337 to 1453.
The Battle of Agincourt (1415) was a famous English victory during the Hundred Years' War.
1348: The Black Death (bubonic plague) reached Britain, killing about one-third of the population.
The Black Death created labor shortages that gave ordinary people more bargaining power.
The Middle Ages saw the building of great cathedrals and the growth of towns and trade.
memory_hook: Remember 1066 (ten-sixty-six) as the year everything changed: Norman William conquered at Hastings, then came castles, the Domesday Book, and eventually Magna Carta limiting royal power in 1215.

questions.prompt: In what year did William the Conqueror defeat King Harold at the Battle of Hastings?
questions.explanation: 1066 is one of the most important dates in British history. William of Normandy defeated King Harold II at the Battle of Hastings and became King of England.
The Domesday Book (1086) was a great survey ordered by William the Conqueror that recorded who owned every piece of land in England and what it was worth.

questions.prompt: What important document did King John sign in 1215?
questions.explanation: The Magna Carta was signed by King John in 1215. It limited the king's power and established that everyone, including the monarch, is subject to the law.

questions.prompt: What devastating disease reached Britain in 1348?
questions.explanation: The Black Death (bubonic plague) reached Britain in 1348 and killed about one-third of the population, causing major social and economic changes.

questions.prompt: At which famous battle in 1415 did the English defeat the French?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Agincourt in 1415 was a famous English victory during the Hundred Years' War with France.

questions.prompt: What system did the Normans introduce where the king owned all land?
questions.explanation: The feudal system meant the king owned all the land and granted it to nobles in return for military service and loyalty.

questions.prompt: Which famous castle in London was built by the Normans?
questions.explanation: The Tower of London was built by the Normans as a fortress and symbol of power. It still stands today as one of London's most famous landmarks.

questions.prompt: How long did the Hundred Years' War between England and France last?
questions.explanation: Despite its name, the Hundred Years' War lasted 116 years, from 1337 to 1453, involving several conflicts between England and France.
